original_abstract: We report the synthesis and characterization of (NBu4)2Fe(III)2(dhbq)3, a three-dimensional network comprising Fe(III) centers bridged by paramagnetic semiquinoid linkers. This material exhibits a room-temperature conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm, ranking among the highest values reported to date for a metal–organic framework (MOF). Through a combination of spectroscopic techniques, slow-scan cyclic voltammetry, and temperature-dependent conductivity and magnetic susceptibility measurements, we demonstrate that this high electronic conductivity originates from ligand mixed-valency. Notably, UV-vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy provides the first evidence of Robin–Day Class II/III mixed valency within a MOF architecture. To explore the role of stoichiometry, we synthesized a reduced analog, Na0.9(NBu4)1.8Fe(III)2(dhbq)3. A comparative analysis reveals how the ratio of ligand redox states dictates the resulting electronic conductivity and magnetic ordering temperature. These findings establish transition metal–semiquinoid frameworks as a versatile platform for engineering tunable, long-range electronic communication in porous solids.
humanized_abstract: We present a new class of transition metal-organic frameworks based on a redox active, semiquinoid ligand and report the synthesis and characterization of the open circuit potential reduction product (NBu4)2Fe(III)2(dhbq)3, a network solid containing Fe(III) centers bridged by paramagnetic semiquinoid radical anions and having a room temperature conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm. The high room temperature conductivity of this material, one of the highest reported for a MOF, along with clear indication of ligand redox activity, the presence of long range electronic coupling indicated by temperature dependent behavior in the slow-scan cyclic voltammograms, and temperature dependent conductivity and magnetic susceptibility, all combined strongly support the presence of ligand mixed-valency. The presence of ligand mixed-valency activity has been confirmed for the first time to our knowledge using UV-vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and shown to be responsible for the high room temperature conductivity of the solid. Reducing the stoichiometry, in the form of the reduced material Na0.9(NBu4)1.8Fe(III)2(dhbq)3, leads to different electronic and magnetic properties, offering further tuning of the properties of transition metal-organic frameworks through the manipulation of ligand redox activity. In general, our results indicate that transition metal–semiquinoid frameworks hold considerable promise as building blocks for the design of porous solids that can be tailored to possess a wide range of electronic functionalities.
